title: The cohomology groups of Hilbert schemes and compactified Jacobians of planar curves
speaker: Luca Migliorini
affiliation: University of Bologna; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: I will first discuss a relation between the cohomology groups (with rational coefficients) of the compactified Jacobian and those of the Hilbert schemes of a projective irreducible curve $C$ with planar singularities, which extends the classical Macdonald formula, relating the cohomology groups of the symmetric product of a nonsingular curve to those of its Jacobian. The result follows from a "Support theorem" for the relative Hilbert scheme family associated with a versal deformation of the curve $C$.

proof_stages.label: Smooth-curve Macdonald formula and relative upgrade
proof_stages.summary: For a smooth projective curve, the cohomology of Picard varieties and symmetric products is governed by \(H^1(C)\) through exterior-power operations, and the same Macdonald formula upgrades over smooth families to an identity of variations of Hodge structure or derived pushforwards.

proof_stages.label: Singular planar replacements and motivating geometry
proof_stages.summary: For integral planar singular curves, compactified Jacobians of rank-one torsion-free sheaves and Hilbert schemes of points replace Picard varieties and symmetric products, with projectivity, local-complete-intersection properties, dense smooth loci, and motivations from Hitchin systems, affine Springer fibers, curve counting, and knot invariants.

proof_stages.label: Locally versal deformation and decomposition-theorem reduction
proof_stages.summary: The singular curve is embedded in a sufficiently large locally versal family whose relative compactified Jacobian and Hilbert schemes have smooth total spaces; over the smooth locus Macdonald's formula applies, while the BBD decomposition theorem extends the relevant local systems as intersection-complex summands and produces a perverse-filtration framework, up to possible extra supports.

proof_stages.label: Irreducible support theorem and filtered Macdonald relation
proof_stages.summary: For irreducible planar curves in such a locally versal family, the support theorem asserts that no additional proper-support summands occur, so the cohomology of the compactified Jacobian and of the Hilbert schemes is related by the same linear-algebra operations on the intersection-complex extensions, with the perverse filtration carrying essential extra information.

proof_stages.label: Reducible correction by strata and subcurve decompositions
proof_stages.summary: In the reducible case one must choose stability conditions for compactified Jacobians, the Hilbert-scheme support theorem fails, and the missing summands are instead described by Hilbert schemes of lower degree attached to decompositions of the central curve into connected subcurves, matching predictions from the Gopakumar-Vafa/Donaldson-Thomas correspondence.

proof_stages.label: Spectral-curve application to Hitchin-type geometry
proof_stages.summary: Applying the generalized Macdonald relation to the family of spectral curves in the total space of a canonical bundle, the relative Hilbert schemes are compared with a fixed surface Hilbert scheme via an affine fibration, yielding cohomological computations for compactified Jacobians consistent with the \(P=W\) conjecture in low degree.

examples.name: Symmetric powers of the projective line
examples.note: The identity \(\operatorname{Sym}^d(\mathbb P^1)=\mathbb P^d\) is used as a consistency check for the denominator in Macdonald's generating series, not as part of the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes the Macdonald generating factor to the case where the symmetric powers of P1 are projective spaces and checks it gives the projective-space cohomology contribution

passes.reason: Sym^d(P^1) is the genus-zero/H^1-free base case, giving projective space, and is used only as a consistency check for Macdonald's formula.

examples.name: Nodal curve sheaves and punctual Hilbert schemes
examples.note: The node illustrates why rank-one torsion-free sheaves need not be line bundles and why Hilbert schemes over singular points have extra geometry such as a \(\mathbb P^1\) of length-two subschemes.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes a nodal curve by exhibiting the node ideal as torsion-free non-invertible and describing the punctual Hilbert schemes as P1s/chains with monomial points

passes.reason: The nodal example is a singular degeneration used to show rank-one torsion-free sheaves need not be invertible and cycles need not determine unique subschemes.
The node is the minimal singular curve case, used to show rank-one torsion-free sheaves need not be line bundles and cycles need not determine subschemes.
The node and punctual Hilbert schemes are collapsed singular local objects used to show the smooth-curve identifications with line bundles/cycles fail.
At a node the ideal sheaf is torsion-free but non-invertible and punctual Hilbert schemes acquire extra P1-chains, showing the smooth-curve identifications fail.
The nodal curve is a singular degeneration where torsion-free sheaves and punctual Hilbert schemes show the smooth identifications with line bundles and cycles fail.
The nodal examples are singular boundary cases used to show that rank-one torsion-free sheaves need not be line bundles and Hilbert schemes need not equal symmetric products.
The node examples show the smooth-curve identifications fail, since rank-one torsion-free sheaves need not be line bundles and punctual Hilbert schemes are nontrivial.
Used to illustrate non-locally-free rank-one sheaves and punctual Hilbert schemes at an ordinary nodal degeneration.
The nodal local model is the minimal boundary singularity and is used to show torsion-free sheaves and punctual Hilbert schemes differ from the smooth analogues.
The nodal singularity is a degenerate curve case whose ideal sheaf and punctual Hilbert schemes show the smooth-curve identifications fail.
Used to show that on a singular nodal curve rank-one torsion-free sheaves need not be line bundles and cycles no longer determine Hilbert-scheme points.
Used to show the smooth-curve identifications fail, via non-invertible node ideals and punctual schemes supported at a singular node.

examples.name: Rational plane curve singularities \(y^p=x^q\), especially \(y^3=x^4\)
examples.note: These singular rational curves illustrate both the difficulty of extracting individual Betti numbers of compactified Jacobians and, later, the need to record the perverse filtration rather than only total Betti numbers.
examples.engagement_reason: he computes structural invariants for y^p=x^q singularities, including the compactified-Jacobian dimension, vanishing of odd Betti numbers, cell-count issues, and the perverse sources of Betti classes for y^3=x^4

passes.reason: The rational monomial cusp y^p=x^q is a singular rational/weighted-homogeneous curve example used for computation of Betti and perverse data, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: One-parameter smoothing of a curve with two nodes
examples.note: This example shows that a smooth total space of curves is not enough to make the relative Hilbert scheme smooth, motivating the stronger locally versal condition already introduced.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses a one-parameter smoothing of a two-nodal curve to compute the tangent-space dimension at the length-two subscheme node1 plus node2 and show the relative Hilbert scheme is singular

passes.reason: The one-parameter smoothing of a two-nodal curve is under-versal and is used to show smoothness of the total curve family is not enough for smooth relative Hilbert schemes.

The one-parameter smoothing of a two-nodal curve shows that a smooth total curve family is not enough to make the relative Hilbert scheme smooth.

examples.name: Two-parameter elliptic degeneration to a banana curve
examples.note: The banana-curve family demonstrates how an apparently missing central component can be supplied by the stalk cohomology of an intersection complex, and why intersection cohomology need not commute with base change.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through the elliptic-to-banana degeneration, tracking the expected decomposition-theorem summands and explaining how an extra component is accounted for by the intersection complex rather than point support

passes.reason: The banana degeneration is a reducible nodal limit of elliptic curves used to defeat the naive expectation that an extra component forces a point-supported summand.
The banana central fiber is a reducible singular limit used to defeat the naive expectation that an extra component forces a skyscraper summand at the origin.
The banana fiber is a reducible nodal limit of elliptic curves used against the naive expectation of an extra point-supported summand.
The banana fiber is the deepest reducible nodal limit where both vanishing cycles collapse, and it disproves the naive expectation of an extra point-supported summand.
The reducible banana central fibre is a degenerate elliptic limit used to refute the naive expected skyscraper-support/base-change behavior.
The banana central fiber is a reducible nodal degeneration used to refute the naive expectation that the extra component must produce a point-supported summand.

examples.name: Conics degenerating to a double line
examples.note: This degeneration visibly forces proper-support summands in the Hilbert-scheme direct image, explaining why the irreducible support theorem cannot simply extend to reducible curves.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the support discussion to conics degenerating to a double line, identifying the Hilbert-scheme length-one family with the curve family and explaining the necessary skyscraper support

examples.name: Spectral curves in the total space of the canonical bundle
examples.note: The spectral-curve family gives an application where the relative Hilbert schemes map as affine fibrations to a fixed surface Hilbert scheme, allowing Betti computations relevant to the \(P=W\) conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the spectral-curve family in the canonical-bundle surface and derives consequences by mapping the relative Hilbert scheme to the surface Hilbert scheme, identifying an affine fibration and comparing Betti numbers
